name: Harris County, Texas
tagline: The most populous county in Texas — ~4.8M residents across the Houston metro — running its technology through Harris County Universal Services (HCUS), with Commissioners Court controlling a ~$2.8B budget and every major IT contract.
overview: Harris County is the third-most-populous county in the U.S. and the largest in Texas, serving roughly 4.8 million residents across the Houston metro. Its central technology organization is Harris County Universal Services (HCUS), which runs IT, cybersecurity, telecommunications, radio, fleet, records management, and the county 311 line through eleven divisions, supporting around 18,000 county employees and roughly 70 departments. Governance sits with a five-member Commissioners Court — the County Judge plus four precinct Commissioners — which adopts the budget and must approve essentially every significant purchase. Day-to-day administration runs through the County Administrator's office, created in 2021. For a Dell SLG seller, the practical map is: HCUS is the technology buying center, the Office of County Administration and Office of Management & Budget shape what gets funded, and Commissioners Court is the gate every deal of size must clear. The county is mid-modernization — replacing its aging JWEB justice platform, standardizing infrastructure, and rationalizing software licensing — which is where ISG, client, and services opportunity concentrates.

budget.intro: Harris County adopted a roughly $2.8B FY2026 budget on September 24, 2025, after a contested process to close an estimated ~$200M gap. Technology is a comparatively small but strategically concentrated line: HCUS's proposed FY26 budget was about $96.1M, including dedicated cybersecurity, a large Microsoft licensing increase, and an EV-fleet pilot. The fiscal year begins October 1.

stats.label: FY2026 county budget
stats.note: Adopted Sep 24, 2025; closed an estimated ~$200M gap

stats.label: HCUS technology budget
stats.note: FY26 proposed for Universal Services (central IT)

stats.label: Cybersecurity line
stats.note: Within HCUS FY26; plus ~$2M for higher Microsoft licensing costs

notes: The budget passed 3-2 — Commissioners Briones, Ramsey, and Garcia in favor; County Judge Hidalgo and Commissioner Ellis opposed — so technology priorities can shift with the court's balance.

HCUS serves ~18,000 county staff and ~4.8M residents across ~11 divisions; named FY26 priorities include standardizing technology, reducing software-license spend, optimizing call centers, and automation.
The largest IT-adjacent spends sit with operating offices (Sheriff, District Attorney, courts, Flood Control); HCUS is the central path, not the only wallet.

initiatives.name: JWEB → integrated justice platform (iJIMS)
initiatives.summary: Harris County is modernizing JWEB, the central judicial system serving 100+ courts and agencies (Sheriff, DA, Public Defender, Juvenile Probation, Pretrial). An RFI (No. MAY71425) for an Integrated Justice Information System closed July 29, 2025, targeting better interagency data sharing, reliability, and CJIS-compliant security. Large justice-infrastructure and data play.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity program build-out
initiatives.summary: HCUS funds a dedicated cybersecurity line (~$2.5M in FY26) and hosted its inaugural Cyber Day during Cybersecurity Awareness Month 2025. Security sits under the CTO/Information Security function — a recurring opportunity for endpoint, CJIS-aligned protection, and resilience.

initiatives.name: Infrastructure standardization & licensing optimization
initiatives.summary: FY26 priorities call out standardizing technology across departments, reducing software-license costs (including a ~$2M Microsoft increase to manage), optimizing call centers, and using automation to streamline processes — a window for consolidation, client refresh, and managed services.

initiatives.name: Fleet electrification pilot
initiatives.summary: FY26 includes a ~$3.9M EV pilot under HCUS, which also owns Fleet Services — relevant for charging, connected-fleet data, and the supporting compute/edge that fleet telemetry implies.

procurement.intro: Harris County buys through the Harris County Purchasing Department, and any award of size must be approved by Commissioners Court. The county is an active user of cooperative vehicles — including Texas DIR Cooperative Contracts — which let it buy pre-competed technology at state pricing without running its own full RFP. Confirm Dell's coverage on the right co-ops before pursuing a deal.

vehicles.name: Harris County Purchasing + Commissioners Court
vehicles.detail: The Purchasing Department runs solicitations and the Purchasing Agent administers contracts, but Commissioners Court approval is the gate for significant awards. Texas interested-parties disclosure applies to court-approved contracts.

vehicles.name: Texas DIR Cooperative Contracts
vehicles.detail: As a local government, Harris County can buy technology off DIR's pre-competed co-op at State of Texas pricing — a primary path to avoid a full county RFP. Verify Dell's DIR contract scope covers the products in play.

vehicles.name: Other purchasing cooperatives
vehicles.detail: The county also leverages cooperatives such as Choice Partners and other interlocal/national co-ops for goods and services — confirm which vehicle a given category is bought through.

vehicles.name: RFI / RFP solicitations
vehicles.detail: Larger or novel programs (e.g., the iJIMS justice platform) run through formal RFI then competitive solicitation; track the Purchasing portal and named procurement contacts early.

powerStructure.name: Lina Hidalgo
powerStructure.title: Harris County Judge
powerStructure.note: Top elected executive and Commissioners Court chair; announced Sept 2025 she will not seek reelection, serving through December 2026 — a leadership transition is coming.

powerStructure.name: Jesse Dickerman
powerStructure.title: County Administrator
powerStructure.note: Appointed April 23, 2025 (4-1 vote); runs day-to-day county administration including budget and operations coordination. Prior role: director of energy transition.

powerStructure.name: Sindhu Menon
powerStructure.title: Executive Director & CIO, Universal Services (HCUS)
powerStructure.note: Leads central IT; the top technology relationship for the account. Harris County's first female CIO (since 2022).

powerStructure.name: Girish Ramachandran
powerStructure.title: CTO, Universal Services
powerStructure.note: Owns technology delivery including the cybersecurity program — key technical buying-center contact alongside the CIO.

powerStructure.name: Commissioners Court
powerStructure.title: County Judge + 4 precinct Commissioners
powerStructure.note: Ellis (Pct 1), Garcia (Pct 2), Ramsey (Pct 3), Briones (Pct 4). Adopts the budget and approves contracts of size — the decision gate for any major deal.

news.title: FY2026-27 budget projected to open with a $129M-$257M shortfall
news.impact: OMB's May 14, 2026 forecast shows another large deficit for the fiscal year starting Oct 1, 2026; Judge Hidalgo is pressing for efficiencies before service cuts, with the proposed budget due ~Aug 17 and adoption ~Sep 17. A second constrained year running — lead hard with cost-optimization, consolidation, and license rationalization; expect scrutiny on any new spend.

news.title: Commissioners Court renews Flock ALPR camera contract (~$869K)
news.impact: Approved May 28, 2026 on a 4-0 vote (Hidalgo abstaining) through June 5, 2027, over heavy public-privacy opposition; court signaled it may revisit privacy protections mid-term. Confirms sustained public-safety data/surveillance investment and the data-governance/retention concerns that surround it — relevant context for storage, data-management, and security positioning.

news.title: Judge Hidalgo proposes county-wide paid mental-health leave policy
news.impact: Brought to Commissioners Court June 2026 to direct offices to draft a mental-health leave policy for ~18,000 county employees not already covered — a workforce/HR-systems signal more than a direct IT buy, but touches HR/benefits process automation.

news.title: Commissioners Court adopts ~$2.8B FY2026 budget
news.impact: Closed an estimated ~$200M gap on a 3-2 vote; sets the FY26 funding envelope (incl. ~$96.1M HCUS) and signals a fiscally constrained year — lead with cost-optimization and consolidation value.

news.title: County Judge Lina Hidalgo announces she won't seek reelection
news.impact: Top-of-county leadership transition lands in Dec 2026 — expect shifts in priorities and the court's balance; build relationships at the HCUS/administration layer that persist across the change.

news.title: Justice modernization RFI (iJIMS) closes
news.impact: RFI No. MAY71425 to replace JWEB across 100+ courts and justice agencies closed July 29, 2025 — track the follow-on solicitation for a large integrated-justice infrastructure and data opportunity.

dellActions: Map the buying center precisely: HCUS (CIO Sindhu Menon, CTO Girish Ramachandran) as the technology owner, Office of County Administration / OMB for funding, and Commissioners Court as the approval gate — sequence the deal accordingly.
Confirm Dell's Texas DIR Cooperative Contract and other co-op coverage so the county can buy at state pricing without a full RFP — the fastest compliant path.
Engage the iJIMS / justice-modernization track early (post-RFI) with integrated-infrastructure, data-sharing, and CJIS-aligned security positioning across the 100+ courts and agencies.
Lead FY26 conversations with cost-optimization, standardization, software-license rationalization, and managed services — the budget is constrained and these are stated county priorities.
Prepare for the Dec 2026 County Judge transition by anchoring relationships at the HCUS and administrator layer that survive changes in the elected court.
